summary: This notebook provides a structured baseline for predicting MCTS agent utility in board games. It demonstrates a comprehensive feature engineering pipeline that parses agent configuration strings, extracts readability metrics from game rules, and constructs numerous ratio-based interactions. The author trains an ensemble of CatBoost regressors using StratifiedGroupKFold grouped by game ruleset to prevent data leakage. Readers should take away the importance of domain-aware feature construction, careful CV stratification, and RMSE-focused post-processing scaling.
competition_flow: Reads CSV data via Polars, applies extensive text parsing, readability metrics, and ratio-based feature engineering, trains two CatBoost models with StratifiedGroupKFold grouped by game ruleset, and averages their clipped predictions for submission.
data_reading: Loaded using polars.read_csv and converted to pandas DataFrames.
data_processing: String cleaning (lowercasing, punctuation removal), outlier clipping on numerical features, memory type casting, TF-IDF vectorization (max_features=500, ngram_range=(2,3)) on rule texts, one-hot encoding with predefined categories, and dropping hundreds of rule/frequency columns.
features_engineering: Agent string parsing (selection, exploration_const, playout, score_bounds), agent position binary features, area/row-col equality, 15+ ratio/interaction features (e.g., Playouts/Moves, ComplexityBalanceInteraction), readability scores (ARI, CLRI, McAlpine_EFLAW) for rule texts, TF-IDF features, one-hot encodings for board/piece properties.
cv_strategy: StratifiedGroupKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=2024) grouped by GameRulesetName
ensembling: Averages predictions from 10 models (2 CatBoost variants × 5 folds), applies a linear scaling factor (×1.1) and clips outputs to [-0.985, 0.985] to minimize RMSE.
insights: Grouping cross-validation by game ruleset is critical for reliable validation scores.
Readability metrics (ARI, CLRI, McAlpine) effectively capture complexity in game rule texts.
Post-processing predictions with scaling and clipping can reduce RMSE even if it slightly increases error on extreme values.
TF-IDF on game rules provides strong signal for agent utility prediction.
critical_findings: The author notes that CV and LB scores can diverge, emphasizing the need for careful validation strategy.
Discretizing the target (round(y*15)) is used to enable stratified grouping by game ruleset.
Clipping predictions to [-0.985, 0.985] improves overall RMSE by balancing errors across the target distribution.
